title: The Informal Economy
summary: The vast world of market activity that happens outside the state's routine tax, licensing, and labor-protection systems — street vendors, cash work, unlicensed workshops, day laborers — which by ILO estimates accounts for the majority of employment globally, challenging the idea that formality is the norm and informality the exception.
hook: On almost any street in Accra, Lagos, or Lima, the economy is fully visible: vendors selling phone credit, mechanics working under open-air awnings, women frying plantains at folding tables. Prices are set, goods change hands, wages are paid. But much of it appears in no tax register or business license — even when labor-force surveys try to measure it. It is real economic life — buying, selling, working — conducted entirely outside the state's ledger. The economist Keith Hart noticed this in Ghana in the early 1970s and gave it a name: the informal economy. His question was not whether this activity was legitimate. It clearly was. His question was why economics had been built as if it did not exist.

beginner.theConcept:
Think about the last time you paid a babysitter in cash. Or bought vegetables from a roadside stand. Money changed hands. But no one filed paperwork. No taxes were paid. No records were kept.

That is the informal economy. It is every sale, every wage, every exchange of goods that happens outside what the government can track. Economists call it informal because it runs without the licenses, registrations, and tax forms that official systems use.

What makes this surprising is how large it is. The International Labour Organization — a United Nations body that monitors work worldwide — has estimated that more than half of all workers globally are employed informally. In parts of Africa and South Asia, the share may be two-thirds or higher. The informal economy is not the exception. In much of the world, it is the rule.
beginner.problemItSolves:
Before the 1970s, most economists treated street vendors and casual workers as a problem. They assumed that countries would move toward formal jobs as they grew richer. Informality was seen as a sign of backwardness that progress would cure.

Keith Hart, an anthropologist working in Ghana, looked more closely. He found that people outside formal jobs were not idle. They ran small shops. They traded goods. They provided services. They had built an economy. Hart coined the phrase informal sector to name it. His point was simple: these people were not outside the economy. They were inside one that economists could not see.

The concept gave researchers a tool. It let them study the huge amount of real economic activity that official numbers were missing.
beginner.howItWorks:
The informal economy runs on trust and cash. Where formal business uses contracts and courts, informal work uses relationships. A vendor gives credit to a regular customer. A landlord rents a room to the cousin of a neighbor. A contractor hires workers he knows by name.

Prices are talked over, not posted on signs. Payment happens now, not at the end of the month. Disputes get settled by community pressure, not by lawyers. This works because the same people deal with each other again and again. Cheating means losing access to the whole network.

For workers, the informal economy offers a living. But it offers little safety. There is no minimum wage, no sick leave, no pension. An injury can wipe out the income with no backup. The informal economy feeds people. It rarely protects them.
beginner.whatItExplains:
The concept explains a puzzle that used to confuse researchers. In many countries, cities grew fast and money poured in. Yet millions of people never entered formal jobs. Why?

Because they were not jobless. They were working in an economy the official numbers could not see.

Hernando de Soto, a Peruvian economist, pointed to another part of this picture. Many families had built real homes on the outskirts of cities. The homes were solid. But they had no legal title. Without title, the owners could not use the home as security to borrow money. De Soto called these assets dead capital — real wealth frozen outside the legal system.

The concept also explains what happens when people move from the countryside to cities. They often land in informal work first. For many, they never leave it. The formal sector is not always waiting for them.
beginner.whereItBreaksDown:
The concept starts to slip when it covers too many different things. A wealthy lawyer who takes cash to dodge taxes and a child hauling bags at a bus station are both in the informal economy. But their lives are almost nothing alike. Putting them under one label makes it harder to understand either one.

There is also a debate about choice. Some workers prefer informal work — its flexibility, its freedom from bosses and schedules. Others are there because no formal job will take them. Both are true at once, in different places and for different people. The single label hides this.

And not everyone in the informal economy is equal. A contractor who hires ten workers without paperwork is in a very different position than those workers. Power matters inside the informal economy, just as it does everywhere else.
beginner.usedAndMisusedC:
Governments have often responded to the informal economy with a simple idea: bring people into the formal system. Give them licenses. Make them pay taxes. Give them legal protections.

In practice this has not always worked. Registration can be slow and expensive. For a small seller, the cost of complying may be higher than the benefit. And if the state does not actually deliver services in return, formal status can feel like a bad deal.

The concept has also been used unfairly. Informal workers are sometimes called tax cheats or criminals. But most are doing the only work they can find. They are not hiding from the system by choice. They were never let in.

general.theConcept:
In 1973, Keith Hart published a study of urban labor markets in Accra that changed how development economists saw the cities of the global South. He found a dense world of traders, artisans, street-food sellers, and casual workers who appeared in no employment records but were clearly doing real work. He called them the informal sector — and the name stuck. The point was not just descriptive. Hart was arguing that these workers were not a leftover from older times. They were a distinct form of economic organization, with its own logic and scale.

The International Labour Organization took up the term immediately. By the 1990s, researchers had found informal employment not just in market stalls but inside factories — piece workers, home-based contractors, seasonal farm workers hired without contracts. What started as a way to see cities in the global South became a way to see labor markets everywhere.
general.problemItSolves:
The concept fixed a gap in development economics. The dominant model of the mid-twentieth century predicted that as countries grew, formal wage jobs would spread and eventually absorb most workers. This prediction did not come true. Decades of growth in South Asia, Africa, and Latin America produced huge informal workforces alongside formal sectors, not instead of them.

Two main explanations compete. One says that too much regulation is the culprit. When formal registration is slow and costly, and when the legal system does not reliably protect small businesses, people find it rational to stay outside. The other says that informal work is what happens when there are more workers than formal jobs — that capital simply does not generate enough formal employment, and informal workers fill the gap. Both explain something. Neither explains everything.
general.howItWorks:
One key difference runs through most research: the gap between those who choose informality and those who are pushed into it. A small business owner who stays unregistered to avoid bureaucratic costs is in a different position from a day laborer who cannot find formal work. The same legal category — unregistered, untaxed, without contract — covers very different situations.

Hernando de Soto's research in Lima in the 1980s captured something important about informal cities. Families who had built homes on unregistered land in hillside neighborhoods held real assets. But those assets were legally invisible. Without title, a home could not be used as collateral. Small businesses could not borrow at bank rates. De Soto called this dead capital — wealth that existed but could not be put to work. His proposed solution was mass titling. Governments in Peru and elsewhere tried it through the 1990s. The results were real but smaller than expected. Legal title helped, but credit access required much more than a piece of paper.
general.whatItExplains:
The concept explains something about how global supply chains actually work. Much of the clothing, electronics, and agricultural produce sold by major international brands rests on informal or semi-formal labor several tiers below the company's name. Home-based piece workers, seasonal farm workers, small subcontractors — these workers are producing for the global economy while standing outside its legal protections.

Jan Breman, a sociologist who spent decades studying labor in South Asia, documented this pattern in detail. He found seasonal migrants moving between construction sites, brick kilns, and farm work — never in one place long enough to gain any legal standing. Breman's term for them was footloose labor. His argument was pointed: these workers were not left behind by economic development. They were being actively used by it, because their lack of legal standing kept their wages low and kept them available.
general.whereItBreaksDown:
The biggest problem with the concept is what it implies about the solution. If the problem is informality, the solution looks like formalization — bring people into the registered economy. But registration is a package. It bundles licensing with taxation, regulation with enforcement. Pressing a street vendor to register may mean adding costs and burdens without delivering the services that formal registration is supposed to guarantee.

Researchers associated with WIEGO — Women in Informal Employment Globalizing and Organizing — have pushed for a different approach. Rather than asking whether work is registered, ask what conditions workers face: their income, their safety, their ability to take sick leave or retire. This shifts the policy question. Instead of moving workers into the formal sector, the goal becomes extending real protections wherever workers actually are. Martha Chen, a leading figure in that network, has argued this case across three decades of work.
general.usedAndMisusedC:
The concept has been pulled in opposite directions by different political camps. Conservatives have sometimes cited it to argue for deregulation — if registration costs were lower, informal businesses would happily join the formal economy, unlocking investment and growth. This idea shaped policy advice from international institutions in the 1990s. Critics pointed out that the barriers were never only regulatory. Lack of market access, thin credit markets, and poor infrastructure kept informal workers from growing regardless of how easy registration was.

From the other direction, labor advocates have used the concept to press for tighter enforcement of formal labor standards. Companies that use subcontracting chains to place workers in informal arrangements are, in effect, escaping the law. The same activity, counted in the same GDP, but with legal protections stripped out at the level of actual workers. The informal economy concept, on this reading, names not a sector but a pattern of cost transfer — from the firm to the worker, and from the employer to the state that eventually bears the cost of unprotected workers.

advanced.theConcept:
The theoretical tension at the center of the informal economy literature is not merely empirical — it concerns what the informal economy is for, analytically. Two major interpretive frameworks have structured most of the debate since the 1990s, and they produce incompatible prescriptions. The first, associated with de Soto and the Institute for Liberty and Democracy, treats the informal economy as a rational market response to state-created barriers: excessive regulation, opaque titling systems, and exclusionary legal institutions drive productive activity underground. On this view, informality is a symptom of too much state, and the cure is legal integration through property rights and regulatory simplification. The second framework, associated with researchers including Jan Breman, Alejandro Portes, and the WIEGO network, treats informality as a permanent feature of labor markets shaped by global capital: informal workers are not escaping state overreach but absorbing risks that formal employers have transferred downward through subcontracting chains, piece work, and casual hiring. On this view, informality is a symptom of insufficient state capacity and worker power, and the cure is extension of protections, not reduction of formality's costs.

These frameworks agree on almost no empirical claim with significant policy implications. They differ on the cause of informality, its relationship to formal capital, the effect of formalization programs, and the welfare of workers within informal arrangements. The debate is not between scholars with different data; it is between scholars with different theoretical priors about how markets and states relate.
advanced.problemItSolves:
The de Soto program, launched influentially in The Other Path (1989) and The Mystery of Capital (2000), made specific empirical predictions. Granting legal title to informal urban property would allow owners to use assets as collateral, accessing credit markets and generating investment capital. Simplifying business registration would encourage formalization of small enterprises, broadening the tax base while extending labor protections. Several governments, notably in Peru, implemented titling programs on this basis during the 1990s.

The evaluation literature is instructive precisely because it does not vindicate either side cleanly. Erica Field's randomized study of the Lima titling program found that formal title increased labor supply by reducing the need for family members to guard property — a real benefit, but not the credit-market effect de Soto predicted. Other studies found that formal title did not consistently produce mortgage access, partly because formal lenders still did not view informal-sector borrowers as creditworthy. The conceptual difficulty the evaluation literature exposed is that de Soto's argument assumed a set of complementary institutions — functioning credit markets, legal enforcement mechanisms, investment opportunities — that titling alone could not produce. Legal formality is a necessary condition for some benefits. It is rarely sufficient.
advanced.howItWorks:
The most analytically productive framing of informality in the recent literature distinguishes between the informal sector (a type of enterprise, characterized by small scale, non-registration, and family labor) and informal employment (a type of labor relationship, characterized by absence of contract, social protection, and legal standing). This distinction, formalized in ILO guidelines around 2003, matters because informal employment is now recognized as widespread within formally registered enterprises — piece workers, home-based contractors, casual workers on daily hire. When a large garment manufacturer in Bangladesh subcontracts cutting and finishing to home workers, those workers are informally employed but are producing for the formal economy. The supply-chain structure makes formality and informality adjacent rather than separate.

This adjacency is the analytical key that the first-generation informal sector literature missed. The informal economy is not a residual category — what is left after formal economic activity is accounted for. It is connected to formal production through subcontracting hierarchies that allow formal firms to externalize risk downward. The workers at the base of those hierarchies bear the income volatility, the absence of benefits, and the health and safety risks that formal employment law would otherwise impose on the firm. Understanding this requires not a taxonomy of sectors but an analysis of the power relations that determine where in a production chain formality ends.
advanced.whatItExplains:
The ILO has estimated — with appropriate hedging given data limitations — that informal employment accounts for roughly sixty percent of global employment when agricultural workers are included, and somewhat lower figures for non-agricultural employment. These estimates are contested methodologically: informal employment is defined partly by what is absent (contract, registration, protection), and national statistical systems vary enormously in how they measure it. The figures should be treated as order-of-magnitude estimates rather than precise measurements. What they establish beyond reasonable dispute is that informal employment is the majority condition for workers in most of the developing world and a significant minority condition in developed economies.

The geographical distribution is not uniform in ways that are theoretically interesting. Informal employment rates tend to be highest in economies with weak state capacity, high inequality, and labor-intensive agricultural sectors. But they are also persistently high in economies with significant formal sectors — India, Brazil, Mexico — in ways that challenge simple modernization predictions. And they have been rising in some developed economies as digital platforms enable new forms of casual employment that reintroduce informal work relations into formally regulated labor markets. The gig economy in the United States and Europe represents, in important respects, a formalization of informality: the legal structure of independent contracting imposed on what are substantively employment relationships, achieving the same risk transfer that characterizes informal employment in the global South through different legal mechanisms.
advanced.whereItBreaksDown:
The confrontation that the informal economy literature has not yet fully worked through is the relationship between informality and state capacity. The de Soto framework treats weak state capacity as an obstacle to formalization and argues for reducing the state's role to accelerate the transition. The WIEGO and labor rights framework treats weak state capacity as a problem requiring state building — extending protections requires a state capable of enforcing them. Both positions are theoretically coherent. The empirical question of which produces better outcomes for informal workers under realistic conditions of limited state capacity has not been settled, partly because the counterfactuals are not observable and partly because the relevant outcomes — income stability, physical security, bargaining power — are not always measurable in the same terms.

There is a harder version of this problem. Some researchers, working in the tradition of James Scott's analysis of state legibility, have argued that informality is partly a deliberate strategy by the poor to remain unreadable to state extraction. On this view, the state's inability to see informal economic activity is not a failure of state capacity but a product of rational evasion by people whose historical experience of the state has been as a tax collector and coercive apparatus rather than a provider of services. If this is right, formalization programs that do not first address the terms on which the state engages with poor communities are likely to fail not because they are technically flawed but because people will continue to prefer invisibility to legibility under those terms. The informal economy is then not merely outside the state's reach — it is a considered distance from it.
advanced.usedAndMisusedC:
The gig economy debate in developed countries represents a contested application of informal economy concepts to formally advanced settings. Platform companies like Uber and Deliveroo classify workers as independent contractors — the legal form of informal self-employment — while exercising algorithmic control over their work conditions in ways that substantively resemble employment. Labor advocates argue this represents the deliberate importation of informal employment relations into regulated markets; companies argue it represents flexible contracting that workers value. Courts in different jurisdictions have reached different conclusions about whether platform work constitutes employment.

What this debate reveals about the informal economy concept is that its defining feature — the absence of formal employment protection — can be reproduced legally within formal institutions as well as through non-registration. The informal economy, on this reading, is not a sector at all. It is a set of labor relations in which risk is borne by workers rather than employers, in which worker protections are absent or unenforceable, and in which the parties to the relationship are not legally or economically equivalent. Those relations can exist in registered enterprises as well as unregistered ones, in rich countries as well as poor ones, in the digital economy as well as the street economy. The concept, properly understood, describes a power relationship — not a place on the map.

foundation.theStory: The informal economy names the large share of economic life that market economies generate but that state accounting systems cannot see. Understanding it requires asking a prior question: why do states have such incomplete pictures of the economies they govern? The answer involves both the limits of state capacity and the rational decisions of workers and small businesses operating under conditions where formal registration costs more than it delivers. The informal economy is an analytical concept developed primarily by development economists and labor researchers to describe a phenomenon that standard national accounting missed — and to ask whether that gap was a measurement failure or a deeper theoretical one.
foundation.theDebate: The central disputes concern whether informality is primarily caused by excessive state regulation (the de Soto position) or by insufficient state capacity and labor power (the labor rights position); whether formalization programs benefit informal workers or expose them to new risks without delivering promised protections; and whether the informal economy is a residual category declining with development or a durable feature of global capitalism that persists and in some respects expands. The empirical evaluation literature on formalization programs has complicated both the optimistic (de Soto) and pessimistic (Breman) positions, finding effects that are more context-dependent than either framework predicted.
foundation.whyItMatters: Understanding the informal economy connects to questions about how development is measured and what it leaves out, the organization of global supply chains, the limits of labor protection in practice, the relationship between legal systems and economic life among the poor, and whether economic formality is always desirable from the perspective of workers as opposed to states. It also clarifies the difference between the informal economy and the household economy: informal work is market activity — money changes hands — just outside the state's registration. The household economy describes non-market reproductive labor. Both are invisible to standard GDP, but for different reasons.
questions: Hernando de Soto argued that extending legal property rights to informal settlements would unlock credit access and economic development. Evaluations of titling programs found more limited effects than de Soto predicted. What does this gap between prediction and outcome reveal about what legal formality can and cannot do for poor communities?

The ILO estimates that informal employment accounts for the majority of global employment, including in countries with large formal sectors like India and Brazil. If informality is this widespread and persistent, does the term still usefully distinguish a phenomenon, or does it simply describe the default condition of labor — one that formality is the exception from?

interpretation.scholarlyConversation: The foundational texts are Hart's 1973 paper and the ILO's first World Employment Programme report the same year, which operationalized Hart's concept for policy use. De Soto's The Other Path (1989) and The Mystery of Capital (2000) constitute the major alternative framework, centering legal barriers and property rights. Jan Breman's fieldwork-based accounts — Footloose Labour (1996) and subsequent work — provide the major empirical counterpoint from a labor sociology perspective. Martha Chen and the WIEGO network have pushed the conversation toward a focus on workers' conditions rather than sector classification. Alejandro Portes and William Haller's contributions to economic sociology formalized the connection between informal and formal economies as a relationship of risk transfer rather than a divide between two separate worlds. James Scott's Seeing Like a State (1998), while not primarily about the informal economy, provides the theoretical resources for understanding informal economic life as partly a deliberate strategy of illegibility toward state power.
interpretation.evidenceAndLimits: The empirical base is uneven. The strongest evidence concerns the prevalence of informal employment — national labor surveys and ILO estimates establish broad orders of magnitude that are not seriously contested, though precise figures vary significantly with definitional choices. The evaluation evidence on formalization programs is stronger for property titling (where randomized studies exist, notably Erica Field's Lima work) than for regulatory simplification (where evidence is more observational). The weakest evidence concerns the welfare effects of informality: whether informal workers are better or worse off than they would be in formal employment depends on comparisons that are not readily observable. Claims about the preferences of informal workers — whether they prefer informal arrangements or are constrained into them — face fundamental identification challenges that survey data cannot fully resolve.
interpretation.takingAPosition: A defensible scholarly position on the informal economy must resist the temptation of either framework's tidiness. The de Soto position that legal barriers explain informality and legal reform will solve it underpredicts the persistence of informality in countries that have implemented titling and deregulation programs. The labor rights position that informal employment is purely a form of exploitation organized by capital underpredicts the diversity of informal arrangements and the evidence that some workers exercise meaningful choice within informal markets. The stronger position holds that informality is best understood as a continuum of labor relations defined by the degree of risk transferred from employers or buyers to workers, and that the relevant policy question is not whether work is registered but whether workers have adequate income, security, and bargaining power. This reframing redirects attention from the legal status of economic activity to the power relations within it — a harder question to answer, but the right one.
questions: James Scott argues that states seek to make populations and their economic activities legible — readable, countable, taxable. Informal economies can be understood as zones of deliberate illegibility, where people prefer to remain outside the state's vision. How does this reading change the policy prescription for informal economy workers compared with frameworks that treat illegibility as a problem to be solved?

The gig economy in developed countries — Uber, Deliveroo, freelance platform work — has been described as an informal economy in formal legal dress: casual labor relations with the risks borne by workers, but mediated through registered corporations. Does the informal economy concept apply to these arrangements? What does it illuminate, and what does it miss, when applied in this context?

research.topic: The scale of informal employment globally
research.content: The ILO's estimates — drawing on national labor force surveys and harmonized definitions established through the 2003 guidelines on informal employment — consistently find that informal employment accounts for roughly sixty percent of global employment when agriculture is included, and somewhat lower proportions in non-agricultural employment. These figures vary substantially by region: South Asia and sub-Saharan Africa have rates typically estimated above seventy percent of employment, while developed economies report ten to twenty-five percent, with those figures reflecting rising platform and casual work. The figures are best understood as order-of-magnitude estimates rather than precise measurements, since national surveys vary in how they measure informal employment and definitions have evolved over time. What the estimates establish beyond reasonable dispute: informal employment is the majority condition for workers in most of the developing world, and formality is the minority condition at the global scale.

research.topic: Informal employment in digital platform economies
research.content: Since roughly 2015, a growing body of economic and legal scholarship has analyzed digital platform work — ride-sharing, food delivery, freelance task platforms — as a form of informal employment within formally registered companies. Workers are classified as independent contractors, receiving no employment benefits, no guaranteed minimum wage, and no protection against dismissal, while platform algorithms exercise significant control over their work. Court decisions in the UK, Netherlands, and Spain have begun reclassifying some platform workers as employees, while US rulings remain inconsistent. The emerging consensus in the research literature is that platform work represents a new legal mechanism for achieving the risk-transfer and cost-reduction functions that characterize informal employment, rather than a genuinely distinct labor category. Whether this represents convergence between developing and developed economy labor markets, or a specific feature of platform business models, remains an active area of research.

research.topic: The welfare preferences of informal workers — constraint versus choice
research.content: The question of whether informal workers prefer their arrangements or are constrained into them is, for most of the global informal workforce, not resolvable with available evidence. What evidence would resolve it: a plausible counterfactual comparing welfare outcomes for observationally similar workers who entered formal and informal employment, derived from a natural experiment or a randomized assignment. Why that evidence is unavailable: the assignment of workers to formal or informal employment is not random — it is correlated with education, location, skill, employer connections, and labor market conditions in ways that make causal inference from observational data unreliable. What can be responsibly inferred: survey evidence from multiple countries finds that informal workers express preferences for formal employment when asked directly; wages and conditions in informal employment are on average below formal equivalents; but some documented populations — particularly own-account workers and micro-entrepreneurs — report higher satisfaction with informal self-employment than comparable formal employment options. What must remain unknowable: the counterfactual welfare of a given worker under the alternative regime they did not experience.

comparativeNarrative.perspective: Development policy and the formalization agenda
comparativeNarrative.name: Bring It Into the Light
comparativeNarrative.content:
For development agencies in the 1990s and 2000s, the informal economy looked like potential going to waste. Hernando de Soto put it starkly: the poor of Lima, Cairo, and Jakarta were sitting on billions of dollars of assets they could not use, because their homes had no legal title. Give them title, simplify registration, and that dead capital would come alive. The World Bank took up this view. Making it easier to register a business became standard development advice.

The theory rested on a real observation. Legal exclusion from property and credit systems was a genuine barrier. What it missed was everything else. Thin credit markets, poor roads, limited market access — these barriers stayed even after registration became easy.

comparativeNarrative.perspective: Labor sociology — work as the foundation
comparativeNarrative.name: The Cost That Capital Avoids
comparativeNarrative.content:
Jan Breman spent decades following seasonal workers across India — construction sites, brick kilns, farm fields. What he found was not a workforce waiting to join the formal sector. It was a population that industry needed precisely because it had no legal standing. These workers were available when demand was high and could be let go when it fell, carrying the income risk that formal employment law would otherwise put on the firm.

On this reading, the informal economy is not a sign of development still in progress. It is how labor-intensive industries keep costs low. The clothing and produce moving through global supply chains rests on workers who hold no contracts at the base. Informal labor does not sit outside the global economy. It helps power it.

comparativeNarrative.perspective: The workers themselves — building from below
comparativeNarrative.name: The Economy They Made
comparativeNarrative.content:
Researchers who spend time in informal markets find something neither framework fully captures. People in the informal economy have built real institutions: rotating credit groups, trade networks based on community trust, apprenticeship systems, vendor associations that negotiate collectively for market space. These are not primitive versions of formal institutions. They are adaptations built for conditions where formal institutions never showed up.

At the same time, WIEGO surveys show that most informal workers — especially women doing home-based piece work — earn below poverty-line wages and have nothing to fall back on when illness or injury strikes. The informal economy is both a site of real creativity and a site of real hardship. Policy that sees only one of these misses what the other explains.

comparativeSummary: Three ways of looking at the informal economy find different things. The formalization view sees locked-up potential — economic energy held back by legal barriers that better rules could remove. The labor view sees a cost-transfer mechanism — informal work as the way industries offload risk onto the people least able to carry it. The view from inside markets sees both: real institutions, real inventiveness, and real deprivation in the same place. What all three reveal together is that the informal economy cannot be understood apart from the formal economy it surrounds and supplies. The two are not parallel tracks that development will eventually merge. Informality absorbs risk, supplies flexibility, and keeps costs low in ways that formal sectors rely on without admitting.

rabbitHole.label: Taxation
rabbitHole.reason: The informal economy is defined by its relationship to taxation: it is the economic life that the tax system cannot see or reach. Understanding why states built registration and revenue infrastructure — and what happens when it cannot function — gives the informal economy its shape and its stakes.

rabbitHole.label: Customs Houses
rabbitHole.reason: Customs houses were early instruments of state legibility over trade — fixed points where goods were counted and taxed. The informal economy grew partly in the spaces beyond that reach, and cross-border informal trade remains one of the most studied dimensions of economic informality in Africa and Latin America.

rabbitHole.label: Debt
rabbitHole.reason: Informal credit — rotating savings groups, moneylenders, community lending circles — is central to how the informal economy functions. The terms on which informal workers can borrow, and the consequences of default without formal legal recourse, connect the informal economy directly to the history and theory of debt.

rabbitHole.label: The Household Economy
rabbitHole.reason: Both the household economy and the informal economy are invisible to standard GDP, but for different reasons. The household economy is unpaid non-market labor — care work that generates no transaction. The informal economy is market activity — buying, selling, wage work — that generates transactions the state simply cannot see. Conflating them obscures what each concept explains.

rabbitHole.label: The Market
rabbitHole.reason: The informal economy is market activity — prices, wages, exchange — organized without formal institutions. Understanding what markets are and how they can function without legal enforcement is the conceptual basis for understanding how informal economies work at all.

rabbitHole.label: Capitalism
rabbitHole.reason: The persistence of informal labor in capitalist economies is not incidental. Labor sociologists argue that informal workers are an integral part of how capitalism manages its costs in labor-abundant settings — available when needed, expendable when not, and cheaper than the law would otherwise allow.

rabbitHole.label: The Gift Economy
rabbitHole.reason: The gift economy operates outside market exchange — things are given, not sold, and reciprocity is social rather than financial. The informal economy is fully a market economy: money changes hands, wages are paid, goods are bought and sold. Holding the two in view sharpens what is distinctive about each form of economic life that falls outside the formal ledger.

reference.author: Hart, Keith
reference.title: Informal Income Opportunities and Urban Employment in Ghana
reference.annotation: The originating text for the informal economy concept — Hart's study of urban labor markets in Accra introduced the term 'informal sector' and reframed casual, unregistered economic activity as a structured phenomenon deserving analysis in its own right, not merely as a symptom of underdevelopment. Every subsequent use of the term traces back to this paper.

reference.author: de Soto, Hernando
reference.title: The Mystery of Capital: Why Capitalism Triumphs in the West and Fails Everywhere Else
reference.annotation: The most influential statement of the property-rights-based theory of informality — de Soto argues that informal assets constitute 'dead capital' held outside legal systems that would allow them to generate further investment. Essential for understanding the policy framework that shaped formalization programs in the 1990s and 2000s, and for understanding the critiques of those programs' limited results.

reference.author: Breman, Jan
reference.title: Footloose Labour: Working in India's Informal Economy
reference.annotation: The essential counterpoint to de Soto: Breman's decades-long fieldwork in Gujarat documents the position of casual, seasonal, and migrant laborers at the base of labor-intensive industries. Establishes that informal employment is not a transitional condition but a durable feature of how formal industries manage labor costs — the baseline text for the labor-centered interpretation of informality.

reference.author: Chen, Martha A.
reference.title: Rethinking the Informal Economy: Linkages with the Formal Economy and the Formal Regulatory Environment
reference.annotation: The conceptual statement of the WIEGO network's reframing of informality around workers' conditions rather than sector classification — argues for shifting from the informal sector (enterprise-level) to informal employment (relationship-level) as the relevant unit of analysis. Supplies the distinction between the informal sector and informal employment that the Advanced layer draws on.

reference.author: ILO (International Labour Organization)
reference.title: Women and Men in the Informal Economy: A Statistical Picture
reference.annotation: The primary source for the entry's ILO estimates on informal employment shares globally and by region. The ILO's third edition of this report, drawing on harmonized national labor force surveys, is the most comprehensive statistical treatment of informal employment at global scale and is the appropriate citation for any quantitative claim about informality's prevalence.

reference.author: Alejandro Portes and William Haller
reference.title: The Informal Economy
reference.annotation: The Handbook of Economic Sociology chapter framing informality as structurally produced by, not separate from, the formal economy — a core theoretical pillar of the Advanced layer.

reference.author: Erica Field
reference.title: Entitled to Work: Urban Property Rights and Labor Supply in Peru
reference.annotation: The Quarterly Journal of Economics study of Peru's urban land-titling program, the empirical basis for the entry's measured (smaller-than-promised) assessment of the de Soto titling thesis.

reference.author: James C. Scott
reference.title: Seeing Like a State: How Certain Schemes to Improve the Human Condition Have Failed
reference.annotation: Scott's account of state legibility as a project — the conceptual frame behind the entry's treatment of the informal economy as a zone of deliberate illegibility.

reference.author: Martha Alter Chen (WIEGO)
reference.title: The Informal Economy: Definitions, Theories and Policies
reference.annotation: The WIEGO working paper synthesizing the dominant schools of thought on informality (dualist, structuralist, legalist, voluntarist); the institutional source for the entry's policy framing.

commerce.author: de Soto, Hernando
commerce.title: The Mystery of Capital: Why Capitalism Triumphs in the West and Fails Everywhere Else
commerce.note: The most influential book on why the informal economy exists and what to do about it. De Soto writes for general readers, and his central image — billions of dollars of assets frozen outside legal systems — is the idea that shaped two decades of development policy and the backlash against it.

commerce.author: Neuwirth, Robert
commerce.title: Shadow Cities: A Billion Squatters, a New Urban World
commerce.note: A journalist's account of years spent living in informal settlements in Rio, Mumbai, Nairobi, and Istanbul. Neuwirth makes the scale and variety of informal urban life concrete in a way that statistics cannot — the place to start if you want to understand the informal economy as lived experience rather than a policy debate.

commerce.author: Portes, Alejandro; Castells, Manuel; Benton, Lauren A.
commerce.title: The Informal Economy: Studies in Advanced and Less Developed Countries
commerce.note: The edited volume that established the informal economy as a global rather than a developing-world phenomenon, with comparative studies across multiple countries and contexts. Good for readers ready to engage the social science behind the concept before moving to primary research.

commerce.author: Bhatt, Ela
commerce.title: We Are Poor but So Many: The Story of Self-Employed Women in India
commerce.note: Bhatt founded SEWA — the Self-Employed Women's Association in India — and this account of decades organizing informal workers is the best single document of what informal work means from inside, and what worker organization can achieve within it. Essential for anyone who wants the view from the ground rather than the view from policy.

commerce.author: Breman, Jan
commerce.title: Footloose Labour: Working in India's Informal Economy
commerce.note: The scholarly foundation for the labor-centered interpretation of informality. Breman's fieldwork across decades in Gujarat is the empirical basis for the argument that informal employment is not transitional but permanent — required reading for serious engagement with the academic debate about what the informal economy actually is and does.

popularCulture.title: City of God (Cidade de Deus, dir. Fernando Meirelles, 2002)
popularCulture.description: Set in a Rio favela, the film shows how drug money fills the gap where formal jobs never came. Violence drives the story, but informal markets are the setting. It gave many viewers their first picture of the informal city. It focuses on the drug trade and leaves out the much larger world of ordinary street work going on just off screen.

popularCulture.title: Salesman (dir. Albert and David Maysles, 1969)
popularCulture.description: Four men sell Bibles door to door. No storefront. No contract. No safety net. The film came out before economists had a name for what it shows. But it captures exactly the informal economy: cash deals, personal trust, and the weight of working without any backup. The camera stays close, and the fatigue of informal work becomes very easy to see.

popularCulture.title: Half of a Yellow Sun — Chimamanda Ngozi Adichie (2006)
popularCulture.description: During the Biafra War, the formal economy stops working. People trade, barter, and find food in markets the state no longer supplies. Adichie shows the informal economy not as a sign of failure but as the system people always fall back on. She makes the work of survival feel ordinary, which is the honest thing — because for most of the world, it is.

popularCulture.title: The gig economy as informal work in new clothes
popularCulture.description: Platform companies in the 2010s sold casual work as freedom. Researchers saw something older: no guaranteed pay, no benefits, all the risk on the worker. Debates about whether delivery drivers are employees or contractors are really the old informal economy debate playing out in app form. The legal wrapping is new. The basic problem is not.

popularCulture.title: Dead capital in development debates
popularCulture.description: Hernando de Soto's phrase spread from economics into political speeches all over the developing world. It gave informal settlements a seat in economic debate they rarely had before. When governments ran titling programs, the results were real but smaller than promised. The phrase traveled further than the policy — which is itself a lesson in how ideas work when they are easy to repeat.
